{{redirect|Yuga|Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada|{{ill|Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|ja|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)}}}} '''Yugasan''' (由加山) is a {{ill|mountain|ja|山岳}}, {{ill|shrine and temple|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}}, and district located in {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, specifically in the Kojima region. The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王}}

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{redirect|Yuga|Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada|{{ill|Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|ja|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)}}}} '''Yugasan''' (由加山) is a {{ill|mountain|ja|山岳}}, {{ill|shrine and temple|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}}, and district located in {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, specifically in the Kojima region. The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王}}
== References == <references />

The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|12=draft}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域|12=draft}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|12=draft}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|12=draft}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来|12=draft}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|12=draft}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|12=draft}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|12=draft}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|12=draft}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|12=draft}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け|12=draft}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇|12=draft}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽|12=draft}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家|12=draft}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|12=draft}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造|12=draft}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|12=draft}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|12=draft}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|12=draft}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|12=draft}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=draft}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|12=draft}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|12=draft}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|12=draft}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|12=draft}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|12=draft}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|12=draft}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|12=draft}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|12=draft}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|12=draft}}

Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|12=draft}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|12=draft}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|12=draft}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=draft}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|12=draft}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|12=draft}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|12=draft}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|12=draft}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|12=draft}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|12=draft}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|12=draft}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|12=draft}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|12=draft}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|薬師如来|" to "|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|"
revision.text:
{{redirect|Yuga|Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada|{{ill|Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|ja|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)|12=draft}}}} '''Yugasan''' (由加山) is a {{ill|mountain|ja|山岳|12=draft}}, {{ill|shrine and temple|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=draft}}, and district located in {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, specifically in the Kojima region. The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|12=draft}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域|12=draft}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|12=draft}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|12=draft}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|12=draft}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現|12=draft}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|12=draft}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|12=draft}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|12=draft}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|12=draft}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け|12=draft}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇|12=draft}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽|12=draft}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家|12=draft}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|12=draft}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造|12=draft}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|12=draft}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|12=draft}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|12=draft}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|12=draft}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=draft}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|12=draft}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|12=draft}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|12=draft}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|12=draft}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|12=draft}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|12=draft}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|12=draft}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|12=draft}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|12=draft}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|12=draft}}
== References == <references />

{{redirect|Yuga|Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada|{{ill|Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|ja|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} '''Yugasan''' (由加山) is a {{ill|mountain|ja|山岳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|shrine and temple|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and district located in {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, specifically in the Kojima region. The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.comment: 49 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:由加山 up to rev 103501062
revision.text:
{{redirect|由加|徳川宗尹側室|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)}} '''由加山'''（ゆがさん）は、[[岡山県]][[倉敷市]][[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]]由加にある[[山岳]]、[[寺社]]および地区。また、その3つを総称したエリア名としても用いられる。通称で'''由加'''（ゆが）とも呼ばれる。かつては'''瑜伽山'''の表記をしていた。ただし、山岳名に関しては「瑜伽山」を正式名称としており、「由加山」は通称となっている。
当地全域は[[瀬戸内海国立公園]]岡山地域に含まれ、[[第二種特別地域]]に指定され'''由加山園地'''として公園事業も行われている。また、風致景観および自然環境の保全や適正な公園利用を目的に、同公園岡山地域を11区域に分けた管理計画区のひとつ（由加山地区）である<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf 風致景観及び自然環境の保全に関する事項及び適正な利用の推進に関する事項|瀬戸内海国立公園岡山地域（PDF）]パンフレット27ページ</ref>。
== 山岳 == {{Infobox 山 | 名称 = 瑜伽（由加）山 | 画像 = | 標高 = 274 | 座標 = | 所在地 = [[岡山県]][[倉敷市]] | 山系 = 由加山系 | 種類 = 丘陵性山地 | OSMズーム = 10 }} 倉敷市児島北部の丘陵地にあり、標高は274m。古来より[[磐座]]信仰・[[山岳信仰]]の対象とされていた山である。正式名称は昔の表記である'''瑜伽山'''（ゆがさん）であり、地図等にも基本的にこの名称でのっている。ただし、一般的には由加山の表記を用いる場合が多い。
[[児島半島]]のほぼ中央部に位置しており、周囲は自然林に覆われているため[[瀬戸内海国立公園]]の一部となっている。[[奈良時代]]（[[733年]]）に[[行基]]がこの山に[[阿弥陀如来]]と[[薬師如来]]の二尊を「'''[[瑜伽大権現]]'''」（ゆがだいごんげん）として祀り、寺社を建てた。瑜伽大権現は、'''[[日本三大権現]]'''の一つとして数えられる<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />。
== 寺社 == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|[[歌川広重]]の描いた由加山]] {{main|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|由加神社本宮}} 現在の'''由加山[[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]]'''と'''[[由加神社本宮]]'''を総称して「由加山」と俗に称されている。日本三大権現の一つである瑜伽大権現の総本山、また厄除けの総本山として知られ、二千有余年の歴史を自称している<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>。
その歴史は、前述の山岳に733年（天平5年）、聖武天皇の勅願により、行基の開基によって経尾山瑜伽寺摩尼珠院（現・蓮台寺）が建立されたとされる。
その後、[[室町時代]]初期に[[増吽]]僧正が中興したのを経て、[[江戸時代]]中期には[[岡山藩]]主[[池田家]]の祈願所となり、正月・五月・九月には藩主自ら由加山に参拝した。 また、蓮台寺境内に池田公が権現造りの本殿・拝殿を新築・寄進した。
元は[[神仏習合]]の施設であったが、明治の[[神仏分離]]の法難にあい、前述の神社の場所だけを由加神社とし、分離。後に各地に分社が建立されたため、由加神社を由加神社本宮と称するようになる。
[[第二次世界大戦後]]の[[国家神道]]解体に伴い、[[宗教法人]]としては形式的に分離したままで神職も配置していたが、事実上蓮台寺により一体的に運営され、僧侶が本殿で祈祷を行うなど明治以前の形態に復していた。
1998年3月、当時の住職と宮司であった兄弟の間で確執が起き、由加神社が神社部分の建物を占拠して独自の宗教法人活動を開始して以降、参拝時に互いの駐車場を共用することを禁止しており、両寺社の関連はないことを主張する看板が立てられるなど、両者の関係は完全に断絶している。また寺側が神社側が占拠した建物の明け渡しを求めるなど裁判沙汰にもなった。
ただし、参拝客は依然として両寺社を総称して「由加山」と称している。
ちなみに、かつてはこの由加山と[[讃岐国]]の[[金刀比羅宮]]の両方に参拝する「[[両参り]]」という風習があったという。『[[東海道中膝栗毛]]』の続編、『宮嶋参詣 続膝栗毛』にも登場している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=厳島参詣膝栗毛<!--[[早稲田大学図書館]]蔵-->|date=不明|publisher=春篁堂|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location=[[大阪]]|volume=上}}</ref>。
== 門前町 == 前述の寺社の参道沿いに参拝客相手の店が発展し、[[門前町]]が形成された。その門前町も'''由加山'''の通称で呼ばれる。現在も往時の雰囲気を残しているが、多くの店は普段は営業を行っておらず、[[正月]]などの参拝客が多い時期にのみ営業している店がほとんどである。
参道沿いは飲食店や土産物店が大半であるが、中には[[郵便局]]などもある。参道沿いから奥には住居が建ち並んでいる。現在も古くからの建造物が多数残っており、参道周辺について規模・構造・デザインが周囲一帯との雰囲気を乱すことのないようにし、老朽化した建造物は門前町景観維持の観点から改修あるいは撤去等を所有者に働きかけるといった、景観保全・町並み保存活動が執り行われている<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />。
また、当地の名物として古くから'''[[あんころ餅]]'''が知られている。
地区から少し離れたところには[[由加温泉]]がある。
かつて両参りが盛んだったころ、由加山南麓の[[田ノ口]]港が最寄の港であったため、田ノ口地区や近接の[[唐琴]]（[[引網村]]）地区や[[下の町]]地区なども門前町として栄えた。現在も田ノ口地区には由加山の[[鳥居]]がある。ちなみに田ノ口という地名は「由加の口」が変化したものである。
== 地域 == {{Infobox Settlement | official_name = 児島由加 | native_name = | settlement_type = [[大字]] | image_skyline = | imagesize = | image_caption = | image_flag = | image_seal = | image_map = | map_caption = 児島由加の位置 | pushpin_map = Japan Okayama | latd=34|latm=29|lats=59.06|latNS=N | longd=133|longm=51|longs=0.67|longEW=E | subdivision_type = [[国の一覧|国]] | subdivision_name = [[ファイル:Flag of Japan.svg|border|25px]] [[日本]] | subdivision_type1 = [[都道府県]] | subdivision_name1 = [[ファイル:Flag of Okayama Prefecture.svg|border|25px]] [[岡山県]] | subdivision_type2 = [[市町村]] | subdivision_name2 = [[ファイル:Flag of Kurashiki, Okayama.svg|border|25px]] [[倉敷市]] | subdivision_type3 = 地域 | subdivision_name3 = 児島 | subdivision_type4 = 地区 | subdivision_name4 = 琴浦 | established_title = | established_date = | government_type = | leader_title = | leader_name = | area_magnitude = | area_total_km2 = | area_land_km2 = | area_urban_km2 = | area_metro_km2 = | area_metro_sq_mi = | population_as_of= [[2014年]]（平成26年）3月31日現在 | population_footnotes = <ref name="jinko">[http://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/dd.aspx?itemid=17023 町名別5歳区切り人口（3ヵ月ごと更新)|倉敷市] 2014年5月31日閲覧。</ref> | population_total = 257 | population_urban = | population_metro = | population_density_sq_mi = | population_density_km2 = | timezone = | utc_offset = | timezone_DST = | utc_offset_DST = | elevation_footnotes= | elevation_m = | elevation_ft = | postal_code_type = [[日本の郵便番号|郵便番号]] | postal_code = 711-0901 | area_code = 086（倉敷MA） | registration_plate = 倉敷 | website = | footnotes = ※座標は琴浦北小学校付近<ref>Google Earthより</ref> }} 由加山は、児島地域の由加地区（児島由加）内に含まれる。
かつて由加地区は、南隣の地区である白尾（しろお）地区（児島白尾）とともに'''山村'''という村名を名乗っており、[[児島郡]]に属していた。その後由加は[[田ノ口村 (岡山県)|田ノ口村]]への移管、[[琴浦町 (岡山県)|琴浦町]]への移行を経て現在倉敷市'''児島由加'''（こじまゆが）となっている。
児島白尾・児島由加を併せて由加山、あるいは由加と通称されることもある。白尾の一部を除き、全域が[[倉敷市立琴浦北小学校]]の学区であることから、琴浦北とも称される。なお、中学校は[[倉敷市立琴浦中学校]]となる<ref>白尾の一部は、[[倉敷市立琴浦東小学校]]。</ref><ref>[http://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=6720 学区一覧表|倉敷市]</ref>。
児島由加の郵便番号は711-0901（[[児島郵便局]]管区）。
=== 沿革 === ; 由加山を含む由加地区の沿革 * [[733年]] - 蓮台寺が建立される * [[江戸時代]] - 山村が岡山藩領となる * [[1871年]][[8月29日]] - [[廃藩置県]]により、岡山県児島郡山村が成立 * [[1889年]][[6月1日]] - [[町村制]]施行により、児島郡山村・木見村・尾原村と合併し、児島郡[[木見村]]となる * [[1906年]][[4月1日]] - 児島郡木見村から旧山村地区が同郡[[田ノ口村 (岡山県)|田ノ口村]]へ移管となる * [[1907年]]4月1日 - 児島郡田ノ口村・[[鴻村]]が合併し、児島郡[[琴浦町 (岡山県)|琴浦村]]となる * [[1915年]][[11月1日]] - 児島郡琴浦村が町制を施行し、同郡[[琴浦町 (岡山県)|琴浦町]]となる * [[1948年]]11月1日 - 児島郡琴浦町が[[児島市]]に編入合併する * [[1967年]][[2月1日]] - 児島市・旧倉敷市・玉島市が対等合併を行い新[[倉敷市]]を新設、由加は倉敷市児島由加となる
=== 施設 === ; 由加山を含む由加地区の施設 * 蓮台寺 * 由加神社本宮 * 普賢茶堂 * 由加神社本宮案内所 * 児島由加簡易郵便局 * [[倉敷由加温泉|倉敷由加温泉ホテル山桃花]] * 由加少年自然の家 ** 由加体育館 * 倉敷市立琴浦北小学校 * 倉敷市立琴浦北幼稚園
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[由加神社本宮]] * [[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]] * [[児島半島]] * [[瀬戸内海国立公園]] * [[あんころ餅]] * [[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]] * [[金刀比羅宮]] * [[阿久良王]]
{{倉敷市の地域}} {{岡山県の町並み保存地区}} {{Japan-geo-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆかさん}} [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:岡山県の山]] [[Category:岡山県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:岡山県の地域]] [[Category:倉敷市の地理]] [[Category:瑜伽信仰]] [[Category:日本の霊峰]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[由加山]] to [[Yuga Mountain]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{redirect|由加|徳川宗尹側室|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)}} '''由加山'''（ゆがさん）は、[[岡山県]][[倉敷市]][[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]]由加にある[[山岳]]、[[寺社]]および地区。また、その3つを総称したエリア名としても用いられる。通称で'''由加'''（ゆが）とも呼ばれる。かつては'''瑜伽山'''の表記をしていた。ただし、山岳名に関しては「瑜伽山」を正式名称としており、「由加山」は通称となっている。
当地全域は[[瀬戸内海国立公園]]岡山地域に含まれ、[[第二種特別地域]]に指定され'''由加山園地'''として公園事業も行われている。また、風致景観および自然環境の保全や適正な公園利用を目的に、同公園岡山地域を11区域に分けた管理計画区のひとつ（由加山地区）である<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf 風致景観及び自然環境の保全に関する事項及び適正な利用の推進に関する事項|瀬戸内海国立公園岡山地域（PDF）]パンフレット27ページ</ref>。
== 山岳 == {{Infobox 山 | 名称 = 瑜伽（由加）山 | 画像 = | 標高 = 274 | 座標 = | 所在地 = [[岡山県]][[倉敷市]] | 山系 = 由加山系 | 種類 = 丘陵性山地 | OSMズーム = 10 }} 倉敷市児島北部の丘陵地にあり、標高は274m。古来より[[磐座]]信仰・[[山岳信仰]]の対象とされていた山である。正式名称は昔の表記である'''瑜伽山'''（ゆがさん）であり、地図等にも基本的にこの名称でのっている。ただし、一般的には由加山の表記を用いる場合が多い。
[[児島半島]]のほぼ中央部に位置しており、周囲は自然林に覆われているため[[瀬戸内海国立公園]]の一部となっている。[[奈良時代]]（[[733年]]）に[[行基]]がこの山に[[阿弥陀如来]]と[[薬師如来]]の二尊を「'''[[瑜伽大権現]]'''」（ゆがだいごんげん）として祀り、寺社を建てた。瑜伽大権現は、'''[[日本三大権現]]'''の一つとして数えられる<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />。
== 寺社 == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|[[歌川広重]]の描いた由加山]] {{main|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|由加神社本宮}} 現在の'''由加山[[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]]'''と'''[[由加神社本宮]]'''を総称して「由加山」と俗に称されている。日本三大権現の一つである瑜伽大権現の総本山、また厄除けの総本山として知られ、二千有余年の歴史を自称している<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>。
その歴史は、前述の山岳に733年（天平5年）、聖武天皇の勅願により、行基の開基によって経尾山瑜伽寺摩尼珠院（現・蓮台寺）が建立されたとされる。
その後、[[室町時代]]初期に[[増吽]]僧正が中興したのを経て、[[江戸時代]]中期には[[岡山藩]]主[[池田家]]の祈願所となり、正月・五月・九月には藩主自ら由加山に参拝した。 また、蓮台寺境内に池田公が権現造りの本殿・拝殿を新築・寄進した。
元は[[神仏習合]]の施設であったが、明治の[[神仏分離]]の法難にあい、前述の神社の場所だけを由加神社とし、分離。後に各地に分社が建立されたため、由加神社を由加神社本宮と称するようになる。
[[第二次世界大戦後]]の[[国家神道]]解体に伴い、[[宗教法人]]としては形式的に分離したままで神職も配置していたが、事実上蓮台寺により一体的に運営され、僧侶が本殿で祈祷を行うなど明治以前の形態に復していた。
1998年3月、当時の住職と宮司であった兄弟の間で確執が起き、由加神社が神社部分の建物を占拠して独自の宗教法人活動を開始して以降、参拝時に互いの駐車場を共用することを禁止しており、両寺社の関連はないことを主張する看板が立てられるなど、両者の関係は完全に断絶している。また寺側が神社側が占拠した建物の明け渡しを求めるなど裁判沙汰にもなった。
ただし、参拝客は依然として両寺社を総称して「由加山」と称している。
ちなみに、かつてはこの由加山と[[讃岐国]]の[[金刀比羅宮]]の両方に参拝する「[[両参り]]」という風習があったという。『[[東海道中膝栗毛]]』の続編、『宮嶋参詣 続膝栗毛』にも登場している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=厳島参詣膝栗毛<!--[[早稲田大学図書館]]蔵-->|date=不明|publisher=春篁堂|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location=[[大阪]]|volume=上}}</ref>。
== 門前町 == 前述の寺社の参道沿いに参拝客相手の店が発展し、[[門前町]]が形成された。その門前町も'''由加山'''の通称で呼ばれる。現在も往時の雰囲気を残しているが、多くの店は普段は営業を行っておらず、[[正月]]などの参拝客が多い時期にのみ営業している店がほとんどである。
参道沿いは飲食店や土産物店が大半であるが、中には[[郵便局]]などもある。参道沿いから奥には住居が建ち並んでいる。現在も古くからの建造物が多数残っており、参道周辺について規模・構造・デザインが周囲一帯との雰囲気を乱すことのないようにし、老朽化した建造物は門前町景観維持の観点から改修あるいは撤去等を所有者に働きかけるといった、景観保全・町並み保存活動が執り行われている<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />。
また、当地の名物として古くから'''[[あんころ餅]]'''が知られている。
地区から少し離れたところには[[由加温泉]]がある。
かつて両参りが盛んだったころ、由加山南麓の[[田ノ口]]港が最寄の港であったため、田ノ口地区や近接の[[唐琴]]（[[引網村]]）地区や[[下の町]]地区なども門前町として栄えた。現在も田ノ口地区には由加山の[[鳥居]]がある。ちなみに田ノ口という地名は「由加の口」が変化したものである。
== 地域 == {{Infobox Settlement | official_name = 児島由加 | native_name = | settlement_type = [[大字]] | image_skyline = | imagesize = | image_caption = | image_flag = | image_seal = | image_map = | map_caption = 児島由加の位置 | pushpin_map = Japan Okayama | latd=34|latm=29|lats=59.06|latNS=N | longd=133|longm=51|longs=0.67|longEW=E | subdivision_type = [[国の一覧|国]] | subdivision_name = [[ファイル:Flag of Japan.svg|border|25px]] [[日本]] | subdivision_type1 = [[都道府県]] | subdivision_name1 = [[ファイル:Flag of Okayama Prefecture.svg|border|25px]] [[岡山県]] | subdivision_type2 = [[市町村]] | subdivision_name2 = [[ファイル:Flag of Kurashiki, Okayama.svg|border|25px]] [[倉敷市]] | subdivision_type3 = 地域 | subdivision_name3 = 児島 | subdivision_type4 = 地区 | subdivision_name4 = 琴浦 | established_title = | established_date = | government_type = | leader_title = | leader_name = | area_magnitude = | area_total_km2 = | area_land_km2 = | area_urban_km2 = | area_metro_km2 = | area_metro_sq_mi = | population_as_of= [[2014年]]（平成26年）3月31日現在 | population_footnotes = <ref name="jinko">[http://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/dd.aspx?itemid=17023 町名別5歳区切り人口（3ヵ月ごと更新)|倉敷市] 2014年5月31日閲覧。</ref> | population_total = 257 | population_urban = | population_metro = | population_density_sq_mi = | population_density_km2 = | timezone = | utc_offset = | timezone_DST = | utc_offset_DST = | elevation_footnotes= | elevation_m = | elevation_ft = | postal_code_type = [[日本の郵便番号|郵便番号]] | postal_code = 711-0901 | area_code = 086（倉敷MA） | registration_plate = 倉敷 | website = | footnotes = ※座標は琴浦北小学校付近<ref>Google Earthより</ref> }} 由加山は、児島地域の由加地区（児島由加）内に含まれる。
かつて由加地区は、南隣の地区である白尾（しろお）地区（児島白尾）とともに'''山村'''という村名を名乗っており、[[児島郡]]に属していた。その後由加は[[田ノ口村 (岡山県)|田ノ口村]]への移管、[[琴浦町 (岡山県)|琴浦町]]への移行を経て現在倉敷市'''児島由加'''（こじまゆが）となっている。
児島白尾・児島由加を併せて由加山、あるいは由加と通称されることもある。白尾の一部を除き、全域が[[倉敷市立琴浦北小学校]]の学区であることから、琴浦北とも称される。なお、中学校は[[倉敷市立琴浦中学校]]となる<ref>白尾の一部は、[[倉敷市立琴浦東小学校]]。</ref><ref>[http://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=6720 学区一覧表|倉敷市]</ref>。
児島由加の郵便番号は711-0901（[[児島郵便局]]管区）。
=== 沿革 === ; 由加山を含む由加地区の沿革 * [[733年]] - 蓮台寺が建立される * [[江戸時代]] - 山村が岡山藩領となる * [[1871年]][[8月29日]] - [[廃藩置県]]により、岡山県児島郡山村が成立 * [[1889年]][[6月1日]] - [[町村制]]施行により、児島郡山村・木見村・尾原村と合併し、児島郡[[木見村]]となる * [[1906年]][[4月1日]] - 児島郡木見村から旧山村地区が同郡[[田ノ口村 (岡山県)|田ノ口村]]へ移管となる * [[1907年]]4月1日 - 児島郡田ノ口村・[[鴻村]]が合併し、児島郡[[琴浦町 (岡山県)|琴浦村]]となる * [[1915年]][[11月1日]] - 児島郡琴浦村が町制を施行し、同郡[[琴浦町 (岡山県)|琴浦町]]となる * [[1948年]]11月1日 - 児島郡琴浦町が[[児島市]]に編入合併する * [[1967年]][[2月1日]] - 児島市・旧倉敷市・玉島市が対等合併を行い新[[倉敷市]]を新設、由加は倉敷市児島由加となる
=== 施設 === ; 由加山を含む由加地区の施設 * 蓮台寺 * 由加神社本宮 * 普賢茶堂 * 由加神社本宮案内所 * 児島由加簡易郵便局 * [[倉敷由加温泉|倉敷由加温泉ホテル山桃花]] * 由加少年自然の家 ** 由加体育館 * 倉敷市立琴浦北小学校 * 倉敷市立琴浦北幼稚園
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[由加神社本宮]] * [[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]] * [[児島半島]] * [[瀬戸内海国立公園]] * [[あんころ餅]] * [[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]] * [[金刀比羅宮]] * [[阿久良王]]

{{redirect|Yuga|Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada|{{ill|Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|ja|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} '''Yugasan''' (由加山) is a {{ill|mountain|ja|山岳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|shrine and temple|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and district located in {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, specifically in the Kojima region. The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

{{redirect|Yuga|Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada|{{ill|Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|ja|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} '''Yugasan''' (由加山) is a {{ill|mountain|ja|山岳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|shrine and temple|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and district located in {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, specifically in the Kojima region. The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
{{redirect|Yuga|Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada|{{ill|Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|ja|由加 (徳川宗尹側室)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga (Concubine of Tokugawa Munetada)|qq=}}}} '''Yugasan''' (由加山) is a {{ill|mountain|ja|山岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mountain|qq=}}, {{ill|shrine and temple|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine and temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}}, and district located in {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki, Okayama|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, specifically in the Kojima region. The term can also refer collectively to all three as an area name. It is commonly called '''Yuga''' (由加). Historically, it was written as '''瑜伽山''', though the mountain's formal name remains "瑜伽山," while "由加山" is a colloquial usage.
The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setonaikai National Park|qq=}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Second-Class Special Zone|qq=}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>
== Mountain == {{Infobox mountain | name = Yuga (瑜伽) Mountain | image = | elevation_m = 274 | coordinates = | location = {{ill|Kurashiki, Okayama|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki, Okayama|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan | range = Yuga Mountain Range | type = Hilly terrain | OSM_zoom = 10 }} Yugasan is a hilly area in northern Kojima, Kurashiki, with an elevation of 274 meters. Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura (Shinto)|qq=}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mountain worship|qq=}}. The official name is '''瑜伽山''' (Yugasan), and it is commonly listed as such on maps, though "由加山" is more frequently used in daily conversation.
Located in the central part of the {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Peninsula|qq=}}, the area is covered in natural forests and is part of Setonaikai National Park. In {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}} (733), the Buddhist monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined statues of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|qq=}} and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}, naming them '''{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}'''. He also established a temple here. Yugasan is counted as one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|ja|日本三大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Great Gongen Shrines|qq=}}.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
== Shrines and Temples == [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Yugasan in Bizan Province - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|280px|''Yugasan in Bizen Province'' by {{ill|Utagawa Hiroshige|ja|歌川広重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utagawa Hiroshige|qq=}}]] {{main|{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|qq=}}|{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Shrine Hongū|qq=}}}} Today, the area collectively known as "Yugasan" includes '''{{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Shrine Hongū|qq=}}'''. It is renowned as the head temple of Yuga Daigongen and as a major {{ill|yakuyoke|ja|厄除け|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yakuyoke|qq=}} (calamity-warding) shrine, claiming a history of over 2,000 years.<ref name="kokuritsukouen"/>
In 733 (Tenpyō 5), under an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Shōmu|qq=}}, Gyōki founded the temple as Keibi-san Yuga-ji Maniju-in (now Rendaiji).
During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the monk {{ill|Zōun|ja|増吽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōun|qq=}} revived the temple. By the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, it became the prayer site for the ruling {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda clan|qq=}} of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|qq=}}, with the domain lord visiting three times annually. The Ikeda clan also built the shrine’s main hall and worship hall in the {{ill|Gongen-zukuri|ja|権現造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongen-zukuri|qq=}} style.
Originally a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} site, it was separated under {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} policies during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, with only the shrine section remaining as Yuga Shrine. Branch shrines were later built, leading to the name change to Yuga Shrine Hongū.
After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the temple and shrine remained formally separate under different religious corporations, though they functioned as a single entity, with Buddhist monks conducting prayers in the main hall.
However, in March 1998, a conflict arose between the resident head monk and head priest, who were brothers. As a result, Yuga Shrine took over the shrine facilities and started operating independently. Since then, both institutions have been completely separate, forbidding shared use of parking spaces and displaying signs emphasizing their separation. Legal disputes ensued, with the temple seeking to reclaim occupied structures.
Despite this, visitors continue to refer to both as "Yugasan."
Historically, worshippers would visit both Yuga Shrine and {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Province|qq=}}, a practice known as "{{ill|Ryōmairi|ja|両参り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryōmairi|qq=}}." This custom is mentioned in the sequel to ''{{ill|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|ja|東海道中膝栗毛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige|qq=}}'', titled ''Miyajima Sankei Zoku Hizakurige''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Miyajima Sankei Hizakurige|date=Unknown|publisher=Shunkōdō|page=16|url=https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/bunko06/bunko06_01003/index.html|accessdate=2021-12-07|location={{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}|volume=Upper}}</ref>
== Monzenmachi == A {{ill|monzenmachi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/monzenmachi|qq=}} (temple town) developed along the approach to the shrine and temple, catering to pilgrims. This area is also referred to as "Yugasan." Many stores operate only during peak visitation periods, such as New Year's.
The approach hosts eateries and souvenir shops, alongside a {{ill|post office|ja|郵便局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post office|qq=}}. Traditional architecture remains, and conservation efforts are in place to maintain the historical streetscape.<ref name="kokuritsukouen" />
The area's specialty is '''{{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ankoro mochi|qq=}}'''.
Nearby is {{ill|Yuga Onsen|ja|由加温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Onsen|qq=}}.
Historically, the {{ill|Tanokuchi Port|ja|田ノ口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanokuchi Port|qq=}} was the nearest port for pilgrims traveling to Yuga Shrine. The port town of Tanokuchi and adjacent {{ill|Karagoto|ja|唐琴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karagoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Shimonochō|ja|下の町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonochō|qq=}} also prospered. Even today, a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torii|qq=}} gate remains in Tanokuchi. The name "Tanokuchi" is said to derive from "Yuga no Kuchi" (由加の口). == See Also == * {{ill|Yuga Shrine Hongū|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Shrine Hongū|qq=}} * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kojima Peninsula|ja|児島半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Peninsula|qq=}} * {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setonaikai National Park|qq=}} * {{ill|ankoro mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ankoro mochi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kojima, Kurashiki|ja|児島地域 (倉敷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima, Kurashiki|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅宮|1=Kotohira-gū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} * {{ill|Agura-ō|ja|阿久良王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agura-ō|qq=}}

The entire area is part of the {{ill|Setonaikai National Park|ja|瀬戸内海国立公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setonaikai National Park|qq=}}, within the Okayama region, designated as a {{ill|Second-Class Special Zone|ja|第二種特別地域|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Second-Class Special Zone|qq=}} and managed under the "Yugasan Park Area" conservation plan.<ref name="kokuritsukouen">[https://chushikoku.env.go.jp/nature/mat/data/m_1/okayama_03.pdf Conservation and Utilization Plan for the Okayama Region of Setonaikai National Park (PDF)] - Page 27</ref>

Since ancient times, it has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura (Shinto)|qq=}} worship and {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mountain worship|qq=}}.
